title: The case for remembering badly
summary: We imagine perfect memory as a gift. The few people who actually have it report something closer to a curse. The essay asks what ordinary, imperfect memory is quietly doing for us.
body:
There is a thought experiment philosophers sometimes use. Imagine a person born with perfect recall — every conversation retained word for word, every face, every smell, every minute of every day, perfectly preserved. Most people, hearing this, react with envy. What a gift, they think. They are wrong.

The few real cases we have suggest something closer to the opposite. People with what is called 'highly superior autobiographical memory' do not, on the whole, describe their condition as a blessing. Many report finding it hard to make decisions, because every old slight, every old failure, returns with full force whenever it is even faintly cued. Forgiveness, in particular, becomes harder, because the memory of the original injury never softens with time the way it does for most of us.

It turns out that ordinary, imperfect memory is doing something we do not normally credit it for. It is editing. Each day, our minds quietly let some of what happened drift out of reach, and round off the rest. This is not a malfunction. It is what allows us to form a coherent self at all. A perfectly remembered life is, paradoxically, harder to live in, because nothing recedes. Everything stays at the front.

There is a second use of forgetting that is even less obvious. Memory, in healthy human beings, is reconstructive rather than playback-based. Each time we recall an event, we are not pressing 'play' on a recording; we are re-assembling a story from fragments. Each re-assembly is slightly different from the last. This shifts the function of memory from preservation to interpretation. It allows us to make sense of what happened in light of what has happened since.

A teenager's memory of her father is not the same as her memory of him at twenty-five, or at fifty, even though it concerns the same person. The 'inaccuracy' here is not a bug. It is the cognitive equipment that lets her keep growing up while keeping him.

None of this argues against caring about truth. There are cases where accurate remembering matters intensely — court testimony, eye-witness reports, the recording of historical events. The point is not that forgetting is always good. The point is that the default assumption — that more accurate memory would be straightforwardly better than the memory we have — turns out, on examination, to be wrong.

We were built to remember roughly. The roughness is part of how we keep moving.

annotations.meaningVi: thuộc về tự truyện, về cuộc đời mình
mang tính tái dựng, tái tạo lại
mặc định, giả định ban đầu

grammarNotes.title: Nominalisation: turning verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn nghị luận học thuật, người viết thường biến động từ và tính từ thành danh từ để tạo cụm danh từ cô đọng, mang tính trừu tượng. Trong bài: 'Memory… is reconstructive rather than playback-based.' Thay vì 'Memory reconstructs events', tác giả dùng tính từ phái sinh để mô tả một đặc tính. Cũng xem: 'The roughness is part of how we keep moving' — tính từ 'rough' biến thành danh từ 'roughness'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In academic essays, writers often turn verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns to build compact, abstract phrases. From the essay: 'Memory… is reconstructive rather than playback-based.' Instead of 'memory reconstructs events', the writer uses an adjective derived from the verb. See also: 'The roughness is part of how we keep moving' — the adjective 'rough' becomes the noun 'roughness'.

There is a second use of forgetting that is even less obvious. Memory, in healthy human beings, is {{b9}} rather than playback-based. Each time we recall an event, we are not pressing 'play' on a recording; we are re-assembling a story from {{b10}}. Each re-assembly is slightly different from the last. blanks.explanation: Most people react with envy to the idea of perfect memory — and, the writer says, they are wrong.
People with perfect autobiographical memory do not, on the whole, describe it as a blessing.
They find it hard to make decisions because every old failure returns with full force.
The memory of the original injury never softens with time — the gentle fading most of us rely on.
Memory is editing — quietly letting some things drift out of reach and rounding off the rest.
Some of what happened is allowed to drift out of reach — a gentle, partial forgetting.
Imperfect memory is what allows us to form a coherent self — a self that hangs together as a story.
Nothing recedes — for perfect memory, nothing falls back, nothing fades, everything stays at the front.
Memory is reconstructive — built each time from fragments, not played back from a recording.
We re-assemble a story from fragments — small pieces, not a complete recording.
Memory shifts from preservation to interpretation — re-telling the past in the light of what came next.
A teenager's memory of her father is not the same as her memory of him at twenty-five, or at fifty — a single concrete example of useful reconstruction.
Court testimony — one example where accurate memory matters intensely.
The default assumption — what we believe without examining — turns out, on closer inspection, to be wrong.
On examination — when looked at closely — the assumption falls apart.
We were built to remember roughly — and the roughness, the writer says, is part of how we keep moving.

translationVi:
Có một thí nghiệm tư duy mà các nhà triết học đôi khi sử dụng. Hãy hình dung một người sinh ra với trí nhớ hoàn hảo — từng cuộc trò chuyện được giữ lại nguyên văn, từng gương mặt, từng mùi vị, từng phút của từng ngày, được lưu giữ hoàn hảo. Hầu hết mọi người, khi nghe thế, phản ứng bằng sự ghen tị. Họ nghĩ: thật là một món quà. Họ đã nhầm.

Vài trường hợp thật sự mà chúng ta biết được lại gợi ý điều gần như ngược lại. Những người có cái được gọi là 'trí nhớ tự truyện siêu cao cấp' không, nhìn chung, mô tả tình trạng của họ là một phước lành. Nhiều người báo cáo rằng họ khó ra quyết định, vì mọi nỗi xúc phạm cũ, mọi thất bại cũ, quay lại với toàn bộ sức nặng mỗi khi nó bị khơi gợi dù chỉ rất nhẹ. Đặc biệt, sự tha thứ trở nên khó khăn hơn, vì ký ức về tổn thương ban đầu không bao giờ mềm đi theo thời gian như với hầu hết chúng ta.

Hóa ra, trí nhớ bình thường, không hoàn hảo, đang làm một việc mà chúng ta thường không ghi công cho nó. Nó đang biên tập. Mỗi ngày, tâm trí chúng ta lặng lẽ để cho một phần những gì đã xảy ra trôi khỏi tầm với, và làm tròn nốt phần còn lại. Đây không phải là một sự cố. Đó là cái cho phép chúng ta hình thành một cái tôi mạch lạc. Một cuộc đời được ghi nhớ hoàn hảo, một cách nghịch lý, lại khó sống hơn — bởi không có gì lùi lại. Mọi thứ đứng nguyên ở phía trước.

Còn một công dụng thứ hai của sự quên mà còn ít hiển hiện hơn. Ở những người khỏe mạnh, trí nhớ mang tính tái dựng, không phải kiểu phát-lại-bản-thu. Mỗi lần ta nhớ một sự kiện, ta không bấm 'play' trên một bản thu; ta đang lắp ráp lại một câu chuyện từ những mảnh. Mỗi lần lắp ráp khác đi một chút so với lần trước. Điều này dịch chuyển chức năng của trí nhớ từ bảo tồn sang diễn giải. Nó cho phép ta hiểu được điều đã xảy ra trong ánh sáng của điều đã xảy ra kể từ lúc đó.

Ký ức của một thiếu niên về cha mình không giống ký ức của cô về ông năm hai mươi lăm tuổi, hay năm năm mươi tuổi, dù vẫn là cùng một con người. Sự 'không chính xác' ở đây không phải lỗi. Đó là bộ máy nhận thức cho phép cô tiếp tục lớn lên mà vẫn giữ ông bên mình.

Không có điều nào trong đây bào chữa cho việc xem nhẹ sự thật. Có những trường hợp nhớ chính xác là vô cùng quan trọng — lời khai trước tòa, báo cáo của nhân chứng, ghi chép sự kiện lịch sử. Quan điểm không phải là quên luôn tốt. Quan điểm là giả định mặc định — rằng trí nhớ chính xác hơn sẽ tốt hơn một cách thẳng băng so với trí nhớ chúng ta đang có — hóa ra, khi xem xét kỹ, lại sai.

Chúng ta được thiết kế để nhớ một cách thô ráp. Sự thô ráp đó là một phần trong cách chúng ta tiếp tục đi tới.

questions.prompt: What thought experiment does the essay open with?
options: Imagining a person born with no memory at all
Imagining a person born with perfect recall — every conversation, face, smell, and minute preserved
questions.explanation: 'Imagine a person born with perfect recall — every conversation retained word for word, every face, every smell, every minute of every day, perfectly preserved.'

questions.prompt: How do most people react to this thought, according to the writer?
options: With envy — they think it would be a gift
questions.explanation: 'Most people, hearing this, react with envy. What a gift, they think. They are wrong.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 1.

questions.prompt: How do people with 'highly superior autobiographical memory' actually describe their condition?
options: Not, on the whole, as a blessing — many report finding it hard to make decisions and harder to forgive
As something they hide from doctors
questions.explanation: 'They do not, on the whole, describe their condition as a blessing.'

questions.prompt: Why does forgiveness become harder for them?
options: Because the memory of the original injury never softens with time the way it does for most people
Because they are angrier by nature
questions.explanation: 'The memory of the original injury never softens with time the way it does for most of us.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say ordinary, imperfect memory is doing for us?
options: Editing — quietly letting some of what happened drift out of reach and rounding off the rest
questions.explanation: 'It is editing. Each day, our minds quietly let some of what happened drift out of reach, and round off the rest.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say a perfectly remembered life would be harder to live in?
options: It would take more space in the brain
Nothing recedes — everything stays at the front, so there is no room for a coherent self
questions.explanation: 'A perfectly remembered life is, paradoxically, harder to live in, because nothing recedes. Everything stays at the front.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: What is the second use of forgetting that the writer identifies?
options: Memory is reconstructive — each recall re-assembles a story from fragments, allowing us to make sense of the past in light of what has happened since
It is faster than perfect memory
questions.explanation: 'Memory… is reconstructive rather than playback-based… It allows us to make sense of what happened in light of what has happened since.'

questions.prompt: What example does paragraph 5 give of reconstructive memory at work?
options: A teenager's memory of her father not being the same as her memory of him at twenty-five or fifty
questions.explanation: 'A teenager's memory of her father is not the same as her memory of him at twenty-five, or at fifty, even though it concerns the same person.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the 'inaccuracy' in the teenager's memory of her father is not a bug?
options: Because it is the cognitive equipment that lets her keep growing up while keeping him
Because she is too young to remember accurately
Because fathers are forgotten more easily
questions.explanation: 'It is the cognitive equipment that lets her keep growing up while keeping him.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 5.

questions.prompt: Is the writer arguing that forgetting is always good?
options: No — he names cases (court testimony, eye-witness reports, historical events) where accurate memory matters intensely
Yes, but only for old people
questions.explanation: 'The point is not that forgetting is always good.'

questions.prompt: What is the writer's actual point in paragraph 6?
options: That the default assumption — more accurate memory would be straightforwardly better — turns out, on examination, to be wrong
questions.explanation: 'The default assumption — that more accurate memory would be straightforwardly better than the memory we have — turns out, on examination, to be wrong.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 6.

questions.prompt: What is the closing image of the essay?
options: 'We were built to remember roughly. The roughness is part of how we keep moving.'
questions.explanation: The final paragraph names roughness as a feature, not a flaw.
questions.hint: Look at the very last paragraph.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to the writer's central claim?
options: Imperfect memory is doing work — editing, reconstructing — that perfect memory would prevent, and that work is part of being a functioning self.
questions.explanation: The whole essay names two specific uses of imperfect memory — editing and reconstruction — and treats them as constitutive.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That people with perfect memory tend not to describe it as a blessing
That ordinary memory is reconstructive rather than playback-based
That accurate memory never matters and should be discouraged
questions.explanation: He names cases — court testimony, eye-witness reports, historical events — where accurate memory matters intensely.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'court testimony, eye-witness reports, the recording of historical events'?
options: He is naming the legitimate exceptions — cases where accurate remembering matters intensely — to keep his argument honest
He is recommending careers in law
questions.explanation: These exceptions stop the essay collapsing into 'forgetting is always good'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer suggests that the slight inaccuracy of our memories of loved ones is what allows us to keep them as we change. Think of someone in your life whose memory you carry — alive or gone. How has your memory of them shifted with time? Would a perfectly preserved recording of them have helped or hindered the relationship you have with that memory now?
